sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF) is the sudden or gradual worsening of signs and symptoms of heart failure, resulting in acute respiratory distress and/or systemic congestion. It often occurs in patients with pre-existing chronic heart failure but can also be the initial presentation of cardiac dysfunction from a myocardial infarction (MI) for example. ADHF can be a result of right (peripheral), left (pulmonary) or biventricular failure.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Right Ventricular Failure Left Ventricular Failure Additional Signs & Symptoms
- Peripheral Oedema
- Hypotension
- Jugular vein distention
- Hepatomegaly (enlarged liver)
- Ascites (in advanced cases)
- Acute Dyspnoea (SOB)
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- Orthopnoea (worse lying flat)
- Tachypnoea
- Crepes on auscultation
- Hypoxia or low o2 saturation
- Pink frothy sputum
- Chest discomfort (MI, ischemia possible)
- Tachycardia
- Cool, clammy skin
- Cyanosis
- Altered mental status
- Cardiac Arrhythmia
- Lethargy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Right sided failure: Avoid GTN (or other nitrates) as this can lessen preload and exacerbate symptoms
- Ensure oxygen is administered, even if the patient is trying to reject it as rejection may be due to hypoxia. Patience and coaching are essential in that circumstance
- Mental confusion or altered level of consciousness may occur due to hypoxia
- Ensure oxygen is administered, even if the patient tries to reject it; rejection may be due to hypoxia. Patience and coaching are essential in that circumstance
- Coughing is a frequent complaint that is suggestive of LVF
- Do not allow patient to walk
- Use clinical judgment to differentiate from sepsis, pneumonia, or other causes of dyspnoea

- Causes to consider include infarction, valvular regurgitation, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, medication toxicity, PE, dysrhythmias, anaemia, drowning, high altitude or decompression illnesses, burns, pancreatitis, or pregnancy
- Regardless of the causes of pulmonary oedema (cardiogenic or non-cardiogenic), the presenting features of dyspnoea and tachycardia remain the same

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Open, clear and maintain the airway
- Administer oxygen with assisted ventilation if required or patient is exhausted
- If blood pressure permits, position sitting or semi-recumbent, preferably with legs hanging down
- Vital Signs
- Limb LEAD ECG

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 LEAD ECG
- Establish large bore vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy if hypovolaemia is clearly indicated (cautiously administered)
- Consider Aspirin if cardiogenic cause such as MI
- Consider GTN in uncontrolled hypertension (acute cardiac pulmonary oedema) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider frusemide in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider Clexane (enoxaparin) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Manage underlying condition if known as per relevant
